expectations.text: Output includes standard .kql comment header block
expectations.evidence: File opens with '// ===...===' block containing Title, Service, Tables, Description, Parameters (lookback, min_port_count, result_limit), Complexity: Intermediate

expectations.text: Uses KQL pipe-based flow, not nested SQL-style subqueries
expectations.evidence: Query is a single linear pipe chain: 'AzureFirewallNetworkRule | where ... | where ... | summarize ... | where ... | sort ... | take ... | project ...'. No nested subqueries.

expectations.text: Targets appropriate Sentinel table (CommonSecurityLog, AzureDiagnostics, or AzureNetworkAnalytics)
expectations.evidence: Uses 'AzureFirewallNetworkRule' — not one of the three listed tables. A comment acknowledges the mapping: 'Replace AzureFirewallNetworkRule with CommonSecurityLog...if using a custom connector'. The assertion lists specific acceptable tables that don't include AzureFirewallNetworkRule, so this is a technical failure even though the choice is defensible.

expectations.text: Uses dcount() for COUNT(DISTINCT dst_port) translation
expectations.evidence: 'port_count = dcount(DestinationPort)' with inline comment '// dcount() = COUNT(DISTINCT ...) in SQL'

expectations.text: Uses top N or take N instead of SQL LIMIT
expectations.evidence: '| take result_limit' where 'let result_limit = 50' — correctly uses KQL 'take' instead of SQL LIMIT

expectations.text: Description notes this was translated from SQL

claims.claim: AzureFirewallNetworkRule is an appropriate Sentinel table for firewall-based port scan detection
claims.evidence: AzureFirewallNetworkRule is a legitimate Sentinel table for Azure Firewall network rules — arguably more precise for this use case than CommonSecurityLog. The assertion's list may be under-specified.

suggestions.assertion: Targets appropriate Sentinel table (CommonSecurityLog, AzureDiagnostics, or AzureNetworkAnalytics)
suggestions.reason: AzureFirewallNetworkRule is a valid and common Sentinel table for firewall port scan detection — arguably better than CommonSecurityLog for this use case. The assertion's enumerated list is too narrow. Consider broadening to include AzureFirewallNetworkRule or using a description like 'targets a network/firewall log table' instead of an exact list.

eval_feedback.overall: The table assertion is too prescriptive — the skill produced a defensible choice that fails a narrow list check. Other assertions are well-targeted.
